input_path: data\papers\Zech et al. - Model-driven Digital Twins for AECO.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A building or infrastructure asset within the Architecture, Engineering, Construction, and Operations (AECO) domain, characterized by complex and heterogeneous environments requiring integration of static models with runtime data to create a virtual representation for simulation, analysis, and optimization across the complete building life cycle from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations during operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Provides real-time monitoring (via real-time insights), predictive analytics, and decision-making support services for infrastructure management in the AECO domain, targeting stakeholders to reduce operational costs and enhance collaboration throughout the building life cycle.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, maintaining temporal consistency for operational monitoring and control without explicit frequency specifications.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: Centralized architecture with the DT Framework acting as a central hub coordinating multiple DT instances, where Building Control Engineers handle device modeling and deployment, Facility Managers manage real-time monitoring and control, and DT Engineers extend capabilities through service integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: Modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom services and analytics, where the DT Framework serves as a central hub facilitating bidirectional data exchange and holistic building life cycle management.
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: Interoperability across heterogeneous systems is addressed through standardized extension points in the modular architecture, supporting integration with external systems and enterprise platforms as part of nonfunctional requirements, though specific data exchange protocols are not detailed.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: BIM-based semantic models for Building Control System (BCS) components (sensors, actuators, controllers), transformed into deployable Digital Twin artifacts via Model-Driven Engineering (MDE). Real-time data exchange synchronizes physical and digital twins, with data sourced from BCS devices managed through automated transformation processes.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Actuators within Building Control Systems (BCS) are modeled within BIM environments. The framework's middleware enables bidirectional communication between the digital twin and physical BCS, allowing remote actuation through automated commissioning protocols and safety-constrained signal transmission.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensors are modeled within BIM environments with configurations specifying spatial distribution and operational parameters. Real-time data collection occurs via Eclipse Hono and Ditto middleware, synchronizing sensor readings from physical infrastructure to the DT for continuous monitoring and analysis.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Validation uses the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric to quantify alignment between digital and physical states, complemented by architectural soundness checks during model transformation. Case studies in Section V assess data synchronization accuracy, model correctness, and real-time fidelity through empirical evaluation protocols.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Eclipse Hono and Ditto enable real-time data exchange capabilities for continuous synchronization between physical building systems and their digital counterparts, facilitating bidirectional transmission of sensor telemetry and operational status data.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Facility Managers issue control commands through the DT interface to Building Control Systems (BCS), with feedback loops confirming command execution status and system responses to ensure accurate physical infrastructure operation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Eclipse Hono and Ditto form the technical network infrastructure, providing IoT connectivity and digital twin management for real-time data exchange between physical assets and the digital twin, with the DT Framework acting as a central hub for bidirectional communication.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The Digital Twin lifecycle comprises initial device modeling within BIM environments by Building Control Engineers to define sensors, actuators, and controllers, followed by Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) transformation to generate structured representations and automatic deployment artifacts; real-time data exchange ensures synchronization with physical systems, interactive visualization enables Facility Manager monitoring and control, and modular extension points support custom service integration for continuous evolution.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The framework employs Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) principles to automatically generate deployable Digital Twin artifacts from enriched BIM models with minimal manual intervention, utilizing a modular architecture with standardized extension points for integrating custom analytics services and extending functionality, thereby enabling dynamic adaptation to evolving operational requirements.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: Interactive visualization interfaces provide Facility Managers with immersive monitoring and control capabilities, while custom analytics services integrated via standardized extension points generate insights to support operational decision-making processes.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The framework utilizes standardized extension points within its modular architecture to facilitate the integration of custom services and analytics; however, specific industry standards or compliance frameworks are not explicitly detailed in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
I. I NTRODUCTION


Digital Twins (DT) are cyber-physical information constructs that through carefully designed integration of static
models with runtime data creates a virtual representation
of a (cyber-)physical asset (e.g., a building) for simulation, analysis, and optimization [1], [2]. Crucially, in the
architecture, engineering, construction and operations (AECO)
domain, DTs enable data-driven infrastructure management
and optimization by providing real-time insights, predictive
analytics, and enhanced decision-making capabilities [3]–[7].
This transformation promises tangible benefits such as reduced
operational costs, enhanced decision-making, and improved
stakeholder collaboration along the complete building life
cycle, from initial design to refurbishments and adaptations
during operation [2], [8]–[12]. However, the realization of this
potential substantially hinges on the ability to systematically
and efficiently deploy DTs in complex and heterogeneous
infrastructure environments [11]–[13].
Despite their promise, the deployment of infrastructure DTs
remains elusive in practice [2]. This is primarily due to
the fragmented nature of models and data, domain-specific


This research has received funding from the Austrian Research Promotion
Agency (FFG) under Grant Agreement No.: 898708, TwinLight, and Grant
Agreement No.: 910225, BOREALIS.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description is highly detailed and directly derived from the introduction. It correctly identifies the AECO domain, the physical assets (buildings/infrastructure), and the core functionality (simulation, analysis, optimization via virtual representations) across the life cycle.

The extraction accurately synthesizes the specific services mentioned in the text, such as real-time monitoring and predictive analytics, while also correctly identifying the benefits for stakeholders like reduced operational costs and enhanced collaboration.

The extraction states 'Not in Document'. However, the source document explicitly mentions several tools and enablers, including 'Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'Eclipse Hono', 'Ditto', 'Unreal Engine', and the 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'. Since evidence exists in the text, a claim of 'Not in Document' is factually incorrect based on the provided source. Relevant quotes: 'grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'leverages Unreal Engine for rich, interactive visualization', 'Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime



configuration management further enhances adaptability by
enabling adjustments without requiring full redeployment.
Complementing these functional requirements, our nonfunctional requirements ( **NF** _X_ ) address critical aspects of
interoperability across heterogeneous systems, scalability from
individual components to entire infrastructure systems, maintainability through low-overhead updates, and accessibility for
users with varying technical expertise.
This requirements-driven approach provides a systematic
foundation for implementing DTs that bridge the gap between
BIM’s static representations and the dynamic operational
needs of the AECO domain, addressing the challenges identified in Section II. At this, we want to emphasize that these
are not all requirements, but rather those pertinent to crafting a
technological spike solution tailored to the specific challenges
and innovations explored in this work (cf. Section V-C). The
discussion section will revisit these requirements to evaluate
their effectiveness and role in advancing the proposed solution.


_B. Solution Proposal_


Figure 1 (p. 6) presents the conceptual model of our
framework, illustrating how the requirements from Table III
are implemented through coordinated roles and system components. Figure 1 demonstrates the flow from enriched BIM
models to operational DTs, emphasizing the key roles and their
contributions to the DT life cycle.
_Building Control Engineers_, using BIM tools, perform
device modeling and initiate the deployment process. _Facility_
_Managers_ then interact with the deployed DT for real-time
monitoring and control. _DT Engineers_ extend the framework’s
capabilities through service integration. The DT Framework
acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange
and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management.


IV. M ODEL  - BASED D EPLOYMENT OF D IGITAL T WINS

It correctly notes that specific frequencies are not provided. Supported by: 'Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts'.

The reference to coordinating multiple instances is supported by the text's mention of 'operational DTs' and the framework's central hub role. Supported by: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub' and the 'Building Control Engineer... Facility Manager... DT Engineer' descriptions in Table I.

Provides a highly specific and detailed description of the system's orchestration, including the modular architecture, standardized extension points, and the central hub pattern facilitating bidirectional exchange and holistic management. Strongly grounded in the text. Supported by: 'The DT Framework acts as a central hub, facilitating bidirectional data exchange and enabling a holistic approach to building life cycle management' and 'framework’s modular architecture, with standardized extension points'.

Correctly associates interoperability with the nonfunctional requirements and the modular architecture's extension points. It accurately reflects the text's focus on heterogeneous systems while noting that specific protocol details are not elaborated upon. Supported by: 'nonfunctional requirements (NFX) address critical aspects of interoperability across heterogeneous systems' and 'standardized extension points to enable modular, service-oriented DT platforms'.

block_3_docs_retrieved:
TABLE I: Actor Roles in our Model-driven DT Deployment
Framework


**Role** **Description**


Building Control Engineer Models and configures devices, defines the control logic, and initiates
DT deployment from BIM models.
Facility Manager Monitors system states, interacts
with the twin, and operates the
physical infrastructure via the DT.
DT Engineer Develops and integrates custom services and plugins to extend DT
functionality.


Commensurate with the limitations of BIM as a seamless

enabler for DTs, we have developed a comprehensive set of
requirements for our model-driven deployment framework.
These requirements emerge from both theoretical considerations and practical insights gained through our research
projects, TwinLight and BOREALIS (cf. Section II).
To address the gap between BIM’s static representations and
the dynamic nature of DTs, our proposal must support the
complete life cycle of DT deployment and operation. Table II
outlines the use cases to be supported by our framework,
spanning from initial device modeling to the development
of custom DT services atop our proposal. These use cases
reflect the core functionality needed by different stakeholders
(cf. Table I) involved in the DT life cycle, including _Building_
_Control Engineers_ who model devices and implement control
logic, _Facility Managers_ who monitor and control building operations, and _DT Engineers_ who extend functionality through
custom services.

The functional requirements ( **F** _X_ ) detailed in Table III form
the conceptual foundation of our solution, beginning with
device modeling capabilities that address BIM’s fundamental
limitation in representing BCS components. This enables
building control engineers to define sensors, actuators, and
controllers directly within the BIM environment, creating a
unified semantic foundation for subsequent transformation
steps. The framework then leverages MDE principles to extract
structured representations from these enriched BIM models
and automatically generate deployable DT artifacts with minimal manual intervention.

Real-time data exchange capabilities ensure continuous synchronization between physical buildings and their digital counterparts, while interactive visualization provides immersive interfaces for monitoring and control. The framework’s modular
architecture, with standardized extension points, supports the
integration of custom services and analytics, allowing DTs
to evolve with changing operational requirements. Runtime

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately captures the core technical contribution of the paper: the use of BIM as a semantic source, Model-Driven Engineering (MDE) for transformation into deployable artifacts, and the integration of Eclipse Hono/Ditto for real-time data synchronization. The evidence supports this: 'present a model-driven framework... grounded in Building Information Modeling (BIM)', 'automates the extraction of domain semantics and the generation of runtime interfaces', and 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange'. The description is specific and well-supported, though it could include more detail on the specific model types (e.g., geometric vs semantic) mentioned in the text.

The description correctly identifies the modeling of actuators in BIM and the role of middleware in bidirectional communication. However, the phrase 'safety-constrained signal transmission' is not explicitly supported by the provided text, which focuses on 'automated commissioning' and 'control strategies'. Quotes: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'middleware for automated commissioning, bidirectional communication between virtual and physical assets'.

The description is highly accurate, covering BIM modeling of sensors, the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for data transmission, and the synchronization process. Quotes: 'define sensors, actuators, and controllers directly within the BIM environment', 'integrates Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange', 'live synchronization between physical assets and digital twins'.

The extraction correctly identifies the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric and its role in evaluating the DT. It also correctly references the case study in Section V and architectural soundness. Quotes: 'evaluate the entanglement of the resulting DT using the Overall Digital Twin Entanglement (ODTE) metric', 'Section V presents the case study and evaluation', 'architectural soundness'.

block_6_docs_retrieved:
[11]. Most frameworks are not designed with AECO-specific
standards like BIM in mind, and those that can be adapted
— such as Bentley’s iTwin.js [32] — rely on proprietary
ecosystems or require substantial manual effort due to a lack
of automation.

Recently, Gerber et al. [33] presented an ad-hoc DT for
a Living Lab for a LEED Platinum academic building based
on BIM for monitoring, simulation, and performance analysis.
Their study emphasizes automated data acquisition, normalization, and 3D visualization, while highlighting challenges
in data integration and stakeholder coordination, insights that
directly inform model-driven approaches in DT engineering.
Their results reinforce earlier findings by Opoku et al. [34]
who also demonstrated the effective integration of DTs with
BIM and IoT platforms to optimize indoor environmental conditions in a university library setting. Both studies underscore
the significance of real-time data utilization and visualization
in enhancing facility management and highlight common challenges in data integration and stakeholder engagement in DT
implementations. In synopsis, AECO adoption is dominated by
commercial tools or custom-built solutions [2], [13]. Notably,
MDE is not present across the surveyed works, leaving a
critical gap in abstraction and reuse capabilities essential for
complex domains like AECO.
Model-driven engineering (MDE) offers a streamlined approach to handling the increasing complexity of systems by
elevating models to primary engineering artifacts [35], [36].
It enables abstraction, formalization, and automation across
the development life cycle, reducing cognitive overload and
improving consistency in large-scale systems [37]. MDE supports the definition of domain-specific modeling languages
and metamodels that unify engineering workflows, providing
a structured foundation for communication across disciplines
and life cycle phases [38], [39]. Importantly, MDE also
strengthens governance by encoding organizational policies
and architectural decisions into formal models, promoting
compliance, reuse, and quality assurance [40]. Recent advances continue to refine the integration of MDE into DT
systems [41]–[47]. As reported in Lehner et al.’s recent survey,
research primarily focuses on runtime adaptability, semantic
coherence, and tool interoperability [48]. Abbasi et al. [49]
examined the phenomenon of _semantic drift_, where the alignment between digital models and their physical counterparts
diverges over time. Their study stresses the necessity of
synchronization techniques to ensure the integrity of modelbased DTs. Kamburjan et al. [50] proposed a declarative life
cycle specification language, enabling dynamic evolution and
rule-based management of DT instances. Meanwhile, Oakes et
al. [51] advanced ontological frameworks to support service

Therefore, the 'Not in Document' classification is accurate.

While the documents mention the use of Eclipse Hono and Ditto for real-time data exchange and mention 'tenant' IDs in configuration metadata, they do not describe cybersecurity protocols, access control mechanisms, authentication systems, or safety-critical considerations. The text focuses on deployment and interoperability rather than specific security measures. Therefore, the 'Not in Document' classification is accurate.
